competitors.label: <head>: The Document Metadata (Header) element
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for the head element, metadata content, and the browser-facing setup that belongs before visible page content.
theirStrengths: MDN is the authoritative reference for what belongs in the head and how browsers interpret metadata, scripts, and styles.
theirGaps: The reference format is less interview-shaped and less explicit about broken viewport, duplicate-title, social-preview, or render-blocking production failures.

realWorldUseCases: Production bugs often come from bad head config: wrong viewport, duplicate titles, broken previews, or render-blocking resources.
followUpConfusion: It configures metadata, resource loading, SEO signals, and social preview behavior before users ever see the UI.

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines the head element precisely, but it stays closer to structure and permitted metadata than to one concrete production-debugging story.
actionableExamples: It includes canonical head examples and the required children, so the example criterion stays competitive rather than flipping fully to us.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the follow-up confusions teams actually hit: viewport bugs, social cards, and render-blocking setup mistakes.

actionableExamples: Keep the essential-head example near the top so the page stays faster to scan than a full reference page.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it packages head metadata around the exact production failures developers usually debug first.

competitors.label: HTML Interview Questions and Answers
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's HTML interview bank is directly relevant because document setup, metadata, linking, and forms all sit inside core HTML interview fundamentals.
theirStrengths: It is a strong HTML interview-prep source with broad coverage across foundational document and markup questions.
theirGaps: The category page is not dedicated to head configuration, so it is less focused on one exact metadata-debugging answer.

realWorldUseCases: Production bugs often come from bad head config: wrong viewport, duplicate titles, broken previews, or render-blocking resources.
followUpConfusion: It configures metadata, resource loading, SEO signals, and social preview behavior before users ever see the UI.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant for HTML interview prep, but the category page is less anchored to broken-head production failures and concrete debug symptoms.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong worked-answer bank, so the example bar stays tied rather than becoming an automatic win for us.
followUpConfusion: Our page answers the likely follow-up more directly by separating visible body content from document-level metadata and loading concerns.

actionableExamples: Keep the control-room framing and one compact head example together so the answer stays interview-ready.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad HTML prep, but our page is more specific about the exact metadata and loading failures that make head-tag answers useful in practice.

competitors.label: Document structure
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev has a direct document-structure article that explains the head-body split, required head components, and why metadata setup matters for browser behavior.
theirStrengths: It is strong on the overall HTML document skeleton and on the practical need for charset, title, and viewport metadata.
theirGaps: It is broader than the exact trivia question because it covers the whole document shell, not only the purpose of the head tag.

realWorldUseCases: Production bugs often come from bad head config: wrong viewport, duplicate titles, broken previews, or render-blocking resources.
followUpConfusion: It configures metadata, resource loading, SEO signals, and social preview behavior before users ever see the UI.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev grounds the head in real document setup, but our answer packages the topic more directly around broken previews, mobile viewport bugs, and render blocking.
actionableExamples: The tutorial includes concrete document examples and required-head guidance, so example quality remains tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more compact on the next questions interviewers ask, especially SEO and social-preview behavior versus visible page content.

actionableExamples: Keep the broken-head mistakes section visible so the page keeps one production-debugging angle, not only structural explanation.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is strong on document structure, but our page is better as a short interview answer because it translates the head tag into concrete SEO, preview, and render-debug consequences.
